results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born 1931) is a Hungarian-born American conceptual artist who is likely the intended identity for 'Agnus Dewes.' Active since the late 1960s and throughout the 1970s, Denes is a pioneer of conceptual, environmental, and ecological art. She was closely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. E.A.T. records from 1969 specifically include Denes as a member who submitted project proposals and biographical materials. Her work often bridged the gap between science, mathematics, and art; she notably participated in the 1970 exhibition 'Software: Information Technology, Its New Meaning for Art' at the Jewish Museum, where she collaborated with the computer scientist group R.E.S.I.S.T.O.R.S. Denes is best known for her 'Philosophical Drawings' and large-scale environmental works such as 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (1968) and 'Wheatfield — A Confrontation' (1982).

results.possibleIdentity: Mike Carson (also known as Michael Carson) was a designer and engineer specialized in diving technology and underwater cinematography equipment, active from the late 1950s through the 1970s and beyond. He is notably linked to the avant-garde art world through a 1958 collaboration with the Fluxus and Happenings artist Al Hansen (a known associate of the Experiments in Art and Technology or E.A.T. circle), with whom he worked on 'wrapping the first pilings' at the Mattson Dock in the Los Angeles Harbor. Carson founded Divetec (later Carson Dive Systems) and was recognized for his technical innovations, including the design of underwater camera housings, lights, and diving helmets like the Carson Mk 3 Air Hat (1970). His expertise brought him to Hollywood, where he designed props and equipment for major film and television productions such as 'Sea Hunt', 'Voyage to the Bottom of the Sea', 'Man from Atlantis', and 'The Poseidon Adventure'.

Mike Carson was an artist and cartoonist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, specifically within the Underground Comics Movement in San Francisco. He is cited as a contributor to the landmark comic 'Ebon' (1970), which was created by Larry Fuller and is recognized as a formative work in Afrofuturism. His participation in the San Francisco counterculture art scene of the time aligns with the era and activities of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Michael Carson (Mike Carson) was a film producer and director active in the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily in Australia. He began his career at the ABC (Australian Broadcasting Corporation) in 1966 and was involved in significant drama productions of the era, including the soap opera 'Bellbird'. He later worked as a producer on various film projects throughout the 1970s and 1980s, including collaborations on films like 'Serpent in the Rainbow' (1973) and 'Spring and Fall' (1980).

results.possibleIdentity: Julius Tobias (1915–1999) was an American abstract painter and sculptor based in New York City, whose career spanned several decades with a peak of activity in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is known for transitioning from Abstract Expressionist paintings in the 1950s to wall-sized white paintings in the late 1960s, and eventually to monumental sculptural environments in the 1970s. His sculptural works typically utilized industrial materials like concrete, steel, and wood to create contained, often barricaded spaces that explored the primacy of space over objects. Tobias was a member of the Art Workers Coalition (AWC) and was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community, specifically collaborating with E.A.T. engineer Per Biorn in 1972. Before his artistic career, he served as a bombardier pilot in the US Air Force during World War II, an experience that influenced his later artistic explorations of sky and vast vistas. Over his career, he was awarded two Guggenheim Fellowships and multiple National Endowment for the Arts grants.
